judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports cover all five expectations with comparable citations and specifics; Report 2 is slightly more explicit about the remediation/impact-ordering issue and folds the SLA-breach disclosure critique in more directly, but the two are essentially equivalent in substance.

report_1.evidence: Per `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md:3-6`: failures began at 14:03, but the first page didn't fire until 14:50 (47 minutes later)

report_2.evidence: Per `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md`: failures began at 14:03, but the first page didn't fire until 14:50 (47 minutes later)

report_1.evidence: `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md:9-10` states 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing, and 37 were unrecoverable and refunded.

report_2.evidence: `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` states 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing, and 37 were unrecoverable and refunded.

report_1.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/sla-commitments.md:3-5` sets the monthly SLA at 99.9%, with any month below that a breach owing service credits. `larkpay-comms/metrics/uptime-2026.csv:6` shows April 2026 at 99.77%, i.e., a confirmed breach.

report_2.evidence: `docs/sla-commitments.md` sets the monthly SLA at 99.9%; April's actual figure of 99.77% (`metrics/uptime-2026.csv`) is below that threshold, meaning this is a contractual breach entitling customers to service credits.

report_1.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md:5` ("Never assign blame to an individual") explicitly bars this framing. The draft should describe the change/process failure, not point at "the on-call engineer."

report_2.evidence: `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1: "Never assign blame to an individual." The draft states "The outage was caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change" — this both names a role and pins fault on a single person.

report_1.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md:7` requires leading with impact and remediation. The current draft opens with an uptime boast (line 3) and closes with more culture-and-track-record language (lines 16-19: "reliability as the first feature," "vast majority of requests... served normally") — this reads as hedging/self-promotion rather than a direct account

report_2.evidence: It opens with an uptime boast and only vaguely references impact ("some checkout requests failed"), never disclosing the real numbers (1,284 failed/reprocessed, 37 unrecoverable refunds) or any remediation for those specific customers... This needs restructuring to lead with what happened to affected customers and what LarkPay is doing to make them whole.
